---
title: "Notebook32"
solutions-release-date: "2026-09-02"
---

### Setup

Run all of the following before starting the notebook.

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
#| eval: false
! wget -q -nc https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/funs.py
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

ub = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/"
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
```

```{python}
#| tags: [remove]
#| include: false
ub = ""
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
prov = funs.read_file(ub + "data/it_province.geojson")
covid = pl.read_csv(ub + "data/it_province_covid.csv")
```

### Questions

This notebook uses two tables. `prov` is a GeoJSON file of Italy's 107
provinces, the administrative unit roughly equivalent to a US county. It has
one row per province with a `province` name, a `geometry` column, and the
`_crs` helper column; the geometries are polygons, not the points from last
time, so each one traces a whole boundary rather than marking a single spot.
`covid` is a daily record of new COVID-19 cases per province from February
2020 through November 2021, with columns `date`, `region`, `province`, and
`cases`. The `province` column is the shared key between the two tables.

As in the last notebook, a few answers get saved back into `prov` — the
projection step and the metric columns built on top of it — because later
questions reuse them. Anything that is not a geometry- or column-building
step should just be printed.

1. In the first block, print `prov`. In the second block, select `province` and
the first 15 characters of each `geometry` string (`c.geometry.str.slice(0, 15)`)
for the first 5 rows to see how the polygons begin. What geometry type are these,
and why can't they be drawn with `geom_point()` the way the city points were?

```{python}
prov
```

```{python}
(
    prov
    .select(c.province, c.geometry.str.slice(0, 15))
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: Every string starts with `MULTIPOLYGON`, not `POINT`. A point is
two numbers, so `geom_point()` has an x and a y to place; a polygon is an
ordered list of many coordinate pairs tracing a closed boundary, and some
provinces are made of several disconnected pieces (the *multi* part), like a
mainland province plus its offshore islands. There is no single (x, y) to
scatter. Drawing these needs `geom_map()`, which knows how to follow the
vertices around each shape.

2. Draw the provinces with `geom_map(fill="white", color="black")` and
`coord_fixed()`, in the raw coordinates the file loaded in.

```{python}
(
    ggplot(prov)
    .geom_map(fill="white", color="black")
    .coord_fixed()
)
```

The file loaded in EPSG:4326, plain longitude and latitude, which you can see
in the `_crs` column from question 1. The outline is recognizably Italy, but
the axis numbers are degrees, and with `coord_fixed()` locking one degree of
longitude to the same length as one degree of latitude, the boot comes out a
little wider and flatter than it should be — at 42°N a degree of longitude
covers noticeably less ground than a degree of latitude, and this plot
pretends they are equal.

3. Before projecting, try to compute each province's area with `prov.geo.area`
while it is still in EPSG:4326. Sort by the new `area` column, smallest first,
and print `province` and `area`. Do the numbers make sense as areas?

```{python}
(
    prov
    .geo.area
    .sort(c.area)
    .select(c.province, c.area)
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: No. The smallest "area" comes out around 0.02, and the largest is
only a few units. These are squared *degrees* of longitude and latitude, not a
real surface area, and a degree is not even a fixed distance — it shrinks as
you move north. Computed this way the numbers are meaningless for comparing
provinces, exactly the trap the chapter warns about: area and distance only
make sense in a projected CRS. Nothing raised an error, though; `geo.area`
happily returned nonsense, which is why the next step projects first.

4. In the first block, project `prov` into EPSG:7791, Italy's national reference
system, and save the result back into `prov`. In the second block, draw it again
with `geom_map(fill="white", color="black")` and `coord_fixed()`, and compare the
shape to question 2.

```{python}
prov = prov.geo.to_crs(7791)
```

```{python}
(
    ggplot(prov)
    .geom_map(fill="white", color="black")
    .coord_fixed()
)
```

**Answer**: The peninsula now stands more upright and slightly narrower; the
flattening from question 2 is gone. The axis numbers are meters instead of
degrees. Projection reshapes polygons more visibly than it moved the city
points in the last notebook, because a polygon's whole outline gets bent, not
just a scatter of dots — the change is most noticeable across the wide
northern provinces, where lines of longitude are converging fastest.

5. Make an interactive map of the provinces with `.geo.explore(tooltip=
["province"])`. Zoom in on a province you can place from memory and check that
its boundary sits where it should.

```{python}
prov.geo.explore(tooltip=["province"])
```

6. Now that `prov` is projected, compute `prov.geo.area` again. In the first
block, save it back into `prov`. In the second block, print the five largest
provinces by area (`province` and `area`, sorted descending). The chapter noted
the units; what are they here?

```{python}
prov = prov.geo.area
```

```{python}
(
    prov
    .sort(c.area, descending=True)
    .select(c.province, c.area)
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: The numbers are now in square meters, the default for a projected
CRS; dividing by 1,000,000 gives square kilometers, so Sassari's roughly
7.7 billion m² is about 7,700 km². The largest provinces are Sassari (northern
Sardinia), Bolzano/Bozen (the Alpine north), and Foggia (in Puglia) — all
sparsely settled and rural. The biggest province is not a big city; that turns
out to matter in the choropleth section below.

7. Print the five *smallest* provinces by area the same way. These are the
ones a national map will barely show.

```{python}
(
    prov
    .sort(c.area)
    .select(c.province, c.area)
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: Trieste, at about 212 km², is the smallest, followed by Prato and
Monza e della Brianza. These are dense urban provinces carved out around a
single city, physically tiny but heavily populated — the mirror image of
Sassari. On a map of the whole country they shrink to a speck, which is the
first hint that visual area and importance can pull in opposite directions.

8. Replace each province's polygon with its centroid using the `centroid`
property (`prov.geo.centroid` — a property, so no parentheses), and print
`province` and the first 12 characters of the new `geometry`. What kind of
geometry is the result now?

```{python}
(
    prov
    .geo.centroid
    .select(c.province, c.geometry.str.slice(0, 12))
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: The `geometry` strings now start with `POINT`: each polygon has
been collapsed to a single point at its geometric center. We did not assign
this back, so `prov` still holds the full polygons. For a compact, roughly
convex province the centroid lands near the middle; for a province shaped like
a crescent or split across islands it can fall outside the province entirely,
which is worth remembering before using a centroid to stand in for a region.

9. In the first block, confirm `province` is a clean key in the polygon table
with `.select(c.province.n_unique(), pl.len())`. In the second block, build a
`total` column — the sum of `cases` per province over the whole period — and
join it onto `prov`. In the third block, make a choropleth with
`geom_map(color="white")`, `coord_fixed()`, and `fill=c.total`.

```{python}
prov.select(c.province.n_unique(), pl.len())
```

```{python}
prov = prov.join(
    covid.group_by(c.province).agg(total = c.cases.sum()),
    on="province",
    how="left"
)
```

```{python}
(
    ggplot(prov, aes(fill=c.total))
    .geom_map(color="white")
    .coord_fixed()
)
```

**Answer**: The key check returns 107 and 107, so every province name is
unique and the left join can match at most one total to each polygon. The map
lights up over Rome, Milan, Turin, and Naples: the totals are dominated by a
handful of big-city provinces, as `total`'s own top rows confirm (Roma
≈305,000, Milano ≈299,000, Napoli ≈288,000).

10. Total cases is an absolute count, and the chapter warns that choropleths of
absolute counts mislead. In the first block, build `dens`, cases per square
kilometer (`c.total / (c.area / 1_000_000)`). In the second block, make the same
choropleth with `fill=c.dens`. How does the picture change, and which map is the
more honest one?

```{python}
prov = prov.with_columns(dens = c.total / (c.area / 1_000_000))
```

```{python}
(
    ggplot(prov, aes(fill=c.dens))
    .geom_map(color="white")
    .coord_fixed()
)
```

**Answer**: The two maps tell different stories. A choropleth fills each
province with a flat color, so a physically large province like Sassari
occupies a lot of the reader's eye no matter how few cases it had, while tiny
Trieste is almost invisible even if it was hit hard. Dividing by area removes
that bias: the density map collapses the big rural south into near-darkness and
makes Naples — small and dense — the brightest region, with the compact
northern provinces around Milan and Monza close behind. The density map is the
more honest one for reading intensity off the page, which is the chapter's rule
of thumb: colors should encode an area-based rate, not a raw total. (The truly
correct normalization for disease would be cases *per person*, but these tables
carry no province population, so per-area is as far as we can honestly go here.)

11. Confirm the density story numerically: sort `prov` by `dens`, descending,
and print the five densest provinces (`province` and `dens`). Does the top of
this list match the bright spots on the map from question 10?

```{python}
(
    prov
    .sort(c.dens, descending=True)
    .select(c.province, c.dens)
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: Naples leads at about 245 cases per km², then Monza e della
Brianza, Milano, Trieste, and Varese — every one of them a small, dense,
urban province, and every one a bright cell on the density map. Naples was only
third by raw total but jumps to first once area is taken out, and Sassari, the
single largest province by area, appears nowhere near the top. That gap between
the two rankings is the whole lesson of the section: on a choropleth, *what you
divide by* decides what the reader sees.
